How Medications and Medication Regimens relate to Patient Mortality

Author

Rebecca Basta and Riley Herber 

Warning: package 'here' was built under R version 4.4.3
Warning: package 'knitr' was built under R version 4.4.3

Authors

  • Rebecca Basta and Riley Herber These authors contributed equally to this work.

Author affiliations

  1. University of Georgia, Athens, GA, USA.

1 Summary/Abstract

Write a summary of your project.

It is recommended to use a structured abstract with Background/Methods/Results/Discussion sections.

2 Introduction

This information is important because it can help healthcare providers better understand the effects of commonly prescribed medications on patient survival. Identifying medications that are associated with improved or worsened outcomes may guide clinical decision-making, optimize treatment strategies, and ultimately improve patient care and safety.

2.1 General Background Information

Provide enough background on your topic that others can understand the why and how of your analysis

2.2 Description of data and data source

This study uses the MIMIC-IV (Medical Information Mart for Intensive Care) database to examine relationships between medication use and patient mortality. MIMIC-IV is a large, publicly available database containing de-identified health records from patients admitted to Beth Israel Deaconess Medical Center in Boston, Massachusetts.

The database includes detailed information on more than 65,000 ICU patients and over 200,000 emergency department visits. Available variables include:

patient demographics

hospital admissions and transfers

laboratory measurements

medication prescriptions and administration records

microbiology cultures

billing and procedure data

mortality outcomes

These data provide a rich resource for investigating relationships between treatment patterns and patient outcomes.

This dataset is then used as a reference to create a synthetic dataset based on the trends present in the real patient data in MIMIC-IV.

2.3 Questions/Hypotheses to be addressed

Our primary objective is to examine how different medications and medication regimens relate to patient mortality. This may include analyzing whether certain medications or combinations of medications are associated with higher or lower mortality rates. To analyze the relationship between medication, use and mortality in the MIMIC-IV dataset, we would first clean and organize medication and outcome data. We would then explore general patterns by comparing mortality rates across patients who received different medications or groups of medications. Simple summaries and visualizations could be used to identify trends or potential associations. This exploratory analysis would help generate initial insights and guide more detailed analyses later on.

3 Methods

3.1 Data Acquisition

Data were obtained from the MIMIC-IV database (version 3.1), a publicly available de-identified electronic health record dataset accessible through PhysioNet. Access to the database requires completion of a data use agreement and credentialing through PhysioNet.

The following tables from the hospital (hosp) module were used:

patients.csv – demographic information including age, sex, and date of death

admissions.csv – hospital admission and discharge information

prescriptions.csv – medication orders associated with each hospital admission

3.2 Data Processing and Cleaning

All data processing and analysis were performed using R. Raw CSV files were imported and cleaned prior to analysis.

Data cleaning steps included:

Removing admissions with a length of stay less than 0 days or greater than 365 days, as these were likely data entry errors.

Standardizing medication names by converting drug names to uppercase and removing extra whitespace.

Assigning medications to 13 pharmacological classes (e.g., antibiotics, opioids, vasopressors) using keyword matching.

Creating binary indicators for each medication class indicating whether at least one medication from that class was prescribed during a given admission.

Full data processing code is documented in:

code/processing-code/mimic-processing.qmd

Exploratory analysis code is available in:

code/eda-code/mimic-eda.qmd

3.3 Statistical Analysis

This study focuses on exploratory, unadjusted analyses.

The following descriptive analyses were conducted:

Calculation of 30-day mortality rates

Distribution of patient ages and demographics

Medication class usage frequencies

Mortality rates stratified by medication class

Relationship between polypharmacy (number of medication classes) and mortality

Results are presented using summary tables and visualizations.

4 Results

4.1 Exploratory/Descriptive analysis

library(here)
knitr::include_graphics(here("results", "figures", "day-30-mortality.png"))

In Figure 1, the vast majority of hospital admissions resulted in survival within 30 days. Only 13 admissions (approximately 0.1%) were associated with death within 30 days, while approximately 99.9% of admissions survived beyond 30 days. This highly imbalanced outcome distribution reflects the synthetic data generation process and indicates that mortality events are rare in the simulated cohort.

knitr::include_graphics(here("results", "figures", "age-distribution-mortality.png"))

Figure 2 The age distribution of patients in the synthetic dataset is approximately bell-shaped, centered around middle-aged to older adults. Most admissions occur between approximately 40 and 80 years of age, with the highest concentration near ages 55–65. Because mortality events are rare in the dataset, the age distribution of patients who died is not clearly distinguishable from those who survived.

knitr::include_graphics(here("results", "figures", "mortality_by_age.png"))

Figure 3 demonstrates that the 30-day mortality rate peaks at 0.2% for individuals in the 55–64 age group. For younger cohorts, specifically those under 40 and those between 40–54, the mortality rate remains constant at 0.1%.

Table 1 shows that females had over double the number of mortality events than males in the 30 day time period.

knitr::include_graphics(here("results", "figures", "perscriptoin-frequency.png"))

Figure 4 shows that the six medication classes shown are all prescribed at similar rates, ranging narrowly from 51.9% to 53.6% of admissions. Anticoagulants are the most frequently used class, appearing in just over half of all admissions (53.6%), followed closely by statins (52.9%) and insulin (52.7%). Antiplatelets, diuretics, and ACE inhibitors round out the list, each prescribed in roughly 52% of cases.

knitr::include_graphics(here("results", "figures", "unadjusted-perscriptoin-frequency.png"))

Figure 5 reveals notably different mortality patterns depending on the medication class. Insulin and ACE inhibitors stand out as the only two classes where patients who received them had lower unadjusted 30-day mortality than those who did not, most strikingly for insulin, where recipients had a mortality rate near 0.05% compared to roughly 0.17% for non-recipients. In contrast, anticoagulants, diuretics, antiplatelets, and statins all show the opposite pattern, with recipients experiencing higher raw mortality rates than non-recipients.

knitr::include_graphics(here("results", "figures", "polypharmacy-frequency.png"))

Figure 6 displays a non-linear, inverted U-shaped relationship between polypharmacy and 30-day mortality. Mortality rises sharply from near 0% among patients receiving only one medication class to a peak of approximately 0.12% to 0.13% among those receiving three classes, then declines steadily through four and five classes before dropping back to near 0% among patients receiving all six classes. The point sizes indicate that the middle groups, particularly those receiving three to four medication classes, represent the largest share of admissions, lending more reliability to the estimates in that range. The near-zero mortality at both extremes is notable and warrants careful interpretation, as patients receiving very few or all six medication classes may represent highly distinct clinical subgroups, and the small sample sizes at those extremes make those estimates less stable. As with the previous figure, Further analysis would be necessary to determine whether the observed pattern reflects a true relationship between polypharmacy and mortality or is driven by differences in underlying patient characteristics across groups.

::: {#tbl-med-mortality-summary .cell tb2-cap=’ Unadjusted 30-day mortality rates by medication class and receipt status, including the number of admissions and observed deaths in each group.’} ::: {.cell-output-display}

received n deaths med_class mort_rate
Not Received 6083 5 Antiplatelet 0.0008220
Received 6674 8 Antiplatelet 0.0011987
Not Received 5920 3 Anticoagulant 0.0005068
Received 6837 10 Anticoagulant 0.0014626
Not Received 6139 8 Ace Inhibitor 0.0013031
Received 6618 5 Ace Inhibitor 0.0007555
Not Received 6095 3 Diuretic 0.0004922
Received 6662 10 Diuretic 0.0015011
Not Received 6030 10 Insulin 0.0016584
Received 6727 3 Insulin 0.0004460
Not Received 6004 6 Statin 0.0009993
Received 6753 7 Statin 0.0010366

::: :::

?@tbl-med-mortality-summary presents the underlying counts behind the unadjusted mortality comparisons. Total admissions are roughly balanced between received and not received groups within each medication class, ranging from approximately 5,900 to 6,800 patients per group, reflecting the near-even prescription rates shown in the earlier figure. Consistent with the previous figure, insulin and ACE inhibitors are the only two classes where recipients had lower mortality rates than non-recipients, with insulin showing the starkest difference (0.00045 vs. 0.00166) and ACE inhibitors showing a more modest gap (0.00076 vs. 0.00130). For anticoagulants, diuretics, and antiplatelets, recipients had notably higher mortality rates than non-recipients. Statins showed the smallest difference between groups, with nearly identical rates regardless of receipt status (0.00104 vs. 0.00100).

To complement the descriptive findings, a series of simple single-predictor logistic regression models were fit to quantify the unadjusted association between each exposure of interest and 30-day mortality. For each of the six medication classes, a separate logistic regression model was fit with the binary medication receipt indicator as the sole predictor of 30-day mortality. This approach provides odds ratios (ORs) and 95% confidence intervals (CIs) that are more interpretable than raw rate differences alone. In addition, logistic regression models were fit for age (continuous) and sex to characterize their individual unadjusted associations with the outcome. Finally, polypharmacy count (number of medication classes received) was modeled as a continuous predictor to formally test the trend observed in the descriptive analysis. All models were fit using the glm() function in R with a binomial family and logit link. Given the highly imbalanced outcome (approximately 0.1% mortality), results should be interpreted cautiously and no p-value threshold is used to designate statistical significance.

library(here)
library(dplyr)
library(broom)
library(knitr)

# Load and prepare the analytic dataset
admissions_clean <- read.csv(here("data", "processed-data", "analysis_dataset.csv")) |>
  rename(
    age               = age_at_admission,
    med_anticoagulant = anticoagulant,
    med_statin        = statin,
    med_insulin       = insulin,
    med_antiplatelet  = antiplatelet,
    med_diuretic      = diuretic,
    med_ace_inhibitor = ace_inhibitor
  ) |>
  mutate(
    n_med_classes = med_anticoagulant + med_statin + med_insulin +
                    med_antiplatelet + med_diuretic + med_ace_inhibitor
  )

# Define predictors to loop over
predictors <- c(
  "med_anticoagulant", "med_statin", "med_insulin",
  "med_antiplatelet", "med_diuretic", "med_ace_inhibitor",
  "age", "gender", "n_med_classes"
)

# Fit one logistic regression per predictor and extract tidy results
basic_results <- lapply(predictors, function(pred) {
  formula <- as.formula(paste("mortality_30day ~", pred))
  fit <- glm(formula, data = admissions_clean, family = binomial(link = "logit"))
  tidy(fit, exponentiate = TRUE, conf.int = TRUE) |>
    filter(term != "(Intercept)") |>
    mutate(predictor = pred)
}) |>
  bind_rows() |>
  select(Predictor = predictor, Term = term,
         OR = estimate, `95% CI Lower` = conf.low,
         `95% CI Upper` = conf.high, `p-value` = p.value) |>
  mutate(across(c(OR, `95% CI Lower`, `95% CI Upper`), \(x) round(x, 3)),
         `p-value` = signif(`p-value`, 3))

# Save and display
saveRDS(basic_results, here("results", "tables", "basic_logreg_results.rds"))
kable(basic_results)
library(here)
library(dplyr)
library(broom)
library(ggplot2)

# Load and prepare the analytic dataset
admissions_clean <- read.csv(here("data", "processed-data", "analysis_dataset.csv")) |>
  rename(
    age               = age_at_admission,
    med_anticoagulant = anticoagulant,
    med_statin        = statin,
    med_insulin       = insulin,
    med_antiplatelet  = antiplatelet,
    med_diuretic      = diuretic,
    med_ace_inhibitor = ace_inhibitor
  ) |>
  mutate(
    n_med_classes = med_anticoagulant + med_statin + med_insulin +
                    med_antiplatelet + med_diuretic + med_ace_inhibitor
  )

predictors <- c(
  "med_anticoagulant", "med_statin", "med_insulin",
  "med_antiplatelet", "med_diuretic", "med_ace_inhibitor",
  "age", "gender", "n_med_classes"
)

basic_results <- lapply(predictors, function(pred) {
  formula <- as.formula(paste("mortality_30day ~", pred))
  fit <- glm(formula, data = admissions_clean, family = binomial(link = "logit"))
  tidy(fit, exponentiate = TRUE, conf.int = TRUE) |>
    filter(term != "(Intercept)") |>
    mutate(predictor = pred)
}) |>
  bind_rows() |>
  select(Predictor = predictor, Term = term,
         OR = estimate, `95% CI Lower` = conf.low,
         `95% CI Upper` = conf.high, `p-value` = p.value) |>
  mutate(across(c(OR, `95% CI Lower`, `95% CI Upper`), \(x) round(x, 3)),
         `p-value` = signif(`p-value`, 3))

basic_results |>
  ggplot(aes(x = OR, y = reorder(Term, OR))) +
  geom_point(size = 3, color = "steelblue") +
  geom_errorbarh(aes(xmin = `95% CI Lower`, xmax = `95% CI Upper`),
                 height = 0.2, color = "steelblue") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 1, linetype = "dashed", color = "gray40") +
  scale_x_log10() +
  labs(
    x = "Odds Ratio (log scale)",
    y = NULL,
    title = "Unadjusted Odds Ratios for 30-Day Mortality"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(panel.grid.minor = element_blank())

ggsave(here("results", "figures", "basic_logreg_forest.png"),
       width = 7, height = 5, dpi = 300)

The unadjusted logistic regression results in Table 2 and Figure 7 are broadly consistent with the descriptive analysis. Insulin receipt and ACE inhibitor receipt are associated with odds ratios below 1.0, suggesting lower unadjusted odds of 30-day mortality among recipients, while anticoagulants, diuretics, antiplatelets, and statins are associated with ORs above 1.0. Age shows a positive association with mortality, as expected, and the polypharmacy count captures the non-linear pattern observed descriptively. Wide confidence intervals throughout reflect the small number of mortality events and reinforce that these estimates are unstable. These unadjusted models are exploratory and should not be interpreted causally.

4.2 Full analysis

To build on the single-predictor models, a multivariable logistic regression was fit to assess the joint association of all six medication classes with 30-day mortality, adjusting simultaneously for age and sex. This model allows each medication class coefficient to be interpreted while holding the other predictors constant, partially addressing the confounding by indication that complicates the unadjusted comparisons. Polypharmacy count was also included as a separate model to test whether the inverted U-shaped association with mortality persists after adjusting for age and sex. Model performance was evaluated using the area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUC-ROC), which is more informative than accuracy for highly imbalanced binary outcomes. Given that only 13 mortality events are present in the synthetic dataset, the multivariable model is severely underpowered—with six medication predictors plus age and sex, the number of events per variable falls well below the recommended threshold of 10. Results should therefore be interpreted as a methodological demonstration of the analytic approach rather than as stable clinical estimates.

library(here)
library(dplyr)
library(broom)
library(knitr)

# Load and prepare the analytic dataset
admissions_clean <- read.csv(here("data", "processed-data", "analysis_dataset.csv")) |>
  rename(
    age               = age_at_admission,
    med_anticoagulant = anticoagulant,
    med_statin        = statin,
    med_insulin       = insulin,
    med_antiplatelet  = antiplatelet,
    med_diuretic      = diuretic,
    med_ace_inhibitor = ace_inhibitor
  ) |>
  mutate(
    n_med_classes = med_anticoagulant + med_statin + med_insulin +
                    med_antiplatelet + med_diuretic + med_ace_inhibitor
  )

# Multivariable model: all six medication classes + age + sex
mv_model <- glm(
  mortality_30day ~ med_anticoagulant + med_statin + med_insulin +
    med_antiplatelet + med_diuretic + med_ace_inhibitor +
    age + gender,
  data = admissions_clean,
  family = binomial(link = "logit")
)

mv_results <- tidy(mv_model, exponentiate = TRUE, conf.int = TRUE) |>
  filter(term != "(Intercept)") |>
  select(Term = term,
         `Adjusted OR` = estimate,
         `95% CI Lower` = conf.low,
         `95% CI Upper` = conf.high,
         `p-value` = p.value) |>
  mutate(across(c(`Adjusted OR`, `95% CI Lower`, `95% CI Upper`), \(x) round(x, 3)),
         `p-value` = signif(`p-value`, 3))

saveRDS(mv_results, here("results", "tables", "multivariable_results.rds"))
kable(mv_results)
library(pROC)
library(ggplot2)

# Generate predicted probabilities (mv_model and admissions_clean carried over from tbl-multivariable)
admissions_clean$pred_prob <- predict(mv_model, type = "response")

# Compute ROC
roc_obj <- roc(admissions_clean$mortality_30day, admissions_clean$pred_prob,
               quiet = TRUE)
auc_val <- round(auc(roc_obj), 3)

# Plot
roc_df <- data.frame(
  specificity = rev(roc_obj$specificities),
  sensitivity = rev(roc_obj$sensitivities)
)

ggplot(roc_df, aes(x = 1 - specificity, y = sensitivity)) +
  geom_line(color = "steelblue", linewidth = 1) +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "gray50") +
  annotate("text", x = 0.7, y = 0.15,
           label = paste0("AUC = ", auc_val), size = 4.5) +
  labs(
    x = "1 - Specificity (False Positive Rate)",
    y = "Sensitivity (True Positive Rate)",
    title = "ROC Curve: Multivariable Logistic Regression"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)

ggsave(here("results", "figures", "roc_curve.png"),
       width = 6, height = 5, dpi = 300)
library(here)
library(dplyr)
library(broom)
library(knitr)

# Load and prepare the analytic dataset
admissions_clean <- read.csv(here("data", "processed-data", "analysis_dataset.csv")) |>
  rename(
    age               = age_at_admission,
    med_anticoagulant = anticoagulant,
    med_statin        = statin,
    med_insulin       = insulin,
    med_antiplatelet  = antiplatelet,
    med_diuretic      = diuretic,
    med_ace_inhibitor = ace_inhibitor
  ) |>
  mutate(
    n_med_classes = med_anticoagulant + med_statin + med_insulin +
                    med_antiplatelet + med_diuretic + med_ace_inhibitor
  )

poly_model <- glm(
  mortality_30day ~ n_med_classes + age + gender,
  data = admissions_clean,
  family = binomial(link = "logit")
)

poly_results <- tidy(poly_model, exponentiate = TRUE, conf.int = TRUE) |>
  filter(term != "(Intercept)") |>
  select(Term = term,
         `Adjusted OR` = estimate,
         `95% CI Lower` = conf.low,
         `95% CI Upper` = conf.high,
         `p-value` = p.value) |>
  mutate(across(c(`Adjusted OR`, `95% CI Lower`, `95% CI Upper`), \(x) round(x, 3)),
         `p-value` = signif(`p-value`, 3))

saveRDS(poly_results, here("results", "tables", "polypharmacy_adjusted.rds"))
kable(poly_results)

The multivariable model results in Table 3 provide adjusted estimates for each medication class after accounting for the others and for age and sex. Comparing these adjusted ORs to the unadjusted ORs from the basic analysis allows informal assessment of how much confounding is present: predictors whose ORs shift substantially toward the null in the adjusted model are likely to have been confounded in the unadjusted analysis. The ROC curve in Figure 8 and its associated AUC summarize overall model discrimination. An AUC near 0.5 would indicate the model performs no better than chance, while values approaching 1.0 indicate strong discrimination; given the severe outcome imbalance and small event count, a modest AUC is expected. The adjusted polypharmacy model in Table 4 tests whether the association between number of medication classes and mortality persists after controlling for age and sex. If the polypharmacy OR attenuates substantially in the adjusted model, this would suggest that the unadjusted trend was partly driven by age or sex differences across polypharmacy groups rather than by medication burden itself. All results from this section should be interpreted as preliminary and methodologically illustrative given the constraints of the synthetic dataset.

5 Discussion

5.1 Summary and Interpretation

Summarize what you did, what you found and what it means.

Discuss what you perceive as strengths and limitations of your analysis.

5.3 Conclusions

What are the main take-home messages?
